turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: The second case I have to announce is Cochise Consultancy Inc. v. United States, ex rel. Hunt, number 18-315. This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. The False Claim Act contains two limitations periods that apply to a 'civil action under §3730' that is, an action asserting that a person presented false claims to the federal government. The first period requires that the action be brought within six years after the statutory violation occurred. The second requires that the action be brought within three years after the United States official charge with the responsibility to act knew or should have known the relevant facts, but not more than 10 years after the violation. Whichever period provides the later date serves as the limitations period. A party who brings a false claim action is known as a Relator. Here respondent, Billy Joe Hunt, is a relator and he filed his action within three years of the date when he allegedly informed federal agents about his claims. Nonetheless, the District Court concluded that Hunt's action was untimely. It reasoned that neither the second statutory period that either the second statutory period does not apply to relator initiated action in which the government decides not to intervene or the three-year period begins when the relator knew the relevant facts. The Court of Appeals reversed, holding that the operative language in §3730(1)(b), a civil action under §3730 encompasses relator-initiated suits, regardless of whether the government intervenes. It also held that the relator is not the official of the United States whose knowledge triggers the second period. In an opinion on filed with the clerk today we affirm. Under petitioner's reading, a relator initiated non-intervene suit is a civil action under §3730 for purposes of the first period but not the second. We do not agree. In general, a single use of a statutory phrase must have a fixed meaning. Nothing about the statute suggests a different result. Moreover, a relator is not an official of the United States. A relator is neither appointed as an officer of the United States nor employed by the United States. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Eleventh Circuit. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
